topic: Scoreboard tab — per-row globe/dome/observation side-by-side with win_by_own_standard + discriminating verdicts (FOUNDATIONAL draft, human-group review)
integration_gate_note: Per operator HNOTE-SCOREBOARD-PIPELINE-ROUTING-001 and queue entry `require_approve_to_proceed: false`, this review is for HUMAN-GROUP CONSUMPTION ONLY. It does NOT authorize integration. Integration is blocked until operator posts HNOTE-SCOREBOARD-INTEGRATION-APPROVED-001.
our_argument_summary: EXP-190 proposes a new top-level `Scoreboard` tab: per-row objects on each WIN/prediction/kill-shot carrying (a) the authoritative globe prediction, (b) the dome prediction AS PUBLISHED by the author, (c) an optional independent formula-check, (d) the observation, and (e) two computed verdicts — win_by_own_standard and discriminating. Phase 1 publishes 15 rows (9 claim_index-classified rows + 6 kill-shots). Load-bearing invariant: never derive dome numbers for the author; blank cells stay blank and are themselves a finding. The draft is structurally sound and the analyst has already anticipated most obvious objections, but there are classification-rule ambiguities and a handful of row-level factual hazards that need tightening before public publication.

proposal_package_review.verdict_flip_plausibility: This is a foundational ADD, not a verdict-flip on existing content. No existing WIN verdict is overturned; the scoreboard is a parallel surface. The only indirect flip is presentational: rows currently rendered with our verdict alone will now also render `win_by_own_standard` (which in many cases will be `yes` — because the author calls it a WIN by his own standard even where our verdict is 'Refuted by Data' or 'Std Model Explains'). Human reviewers should decide whether this dual-verdict surface on the same row is clarifying (two honest columns) or confusing (readers may anchor on `win_by_own_standard=yes` and miss our verdict). Plausibility: high for the ADD; medium-high that the UX risk is adequately mitigated by the intro paragraph.
proposal_package_review.new_tag_naming: Four provenance tags: `forward_dome_native`, `forward_with_globe_inputs`, `fit`, `unclear`. Names are descriptive but operationally subjective — see Hole #1 below. Two outcome columns (`win_by_own_standard`, `discriminating`) with five values each (yes/no/blank/pending/disputed) — `disputed` is overloaded (see Hole #4). Tab label options 'Scoreboard' vs 'Both-Model Predictions' — analyst chose Scoreboard; connotation risk (winners/losers framing undercuts the 'we do not collapse to binary' principle) is real but manageable via intro copy. 'Both-Model Predictions' is more neutral and academically honest but weaker as a nav label.
proposal_package_review.alternatives_considered: Analyst explicitly addresses 8 anticipated objections and 5 counter-considerations inline, which is strong. Missing alternative: author-fill-the-cells-with-plausible-numbers response. Objection #5 handles the rhetorical side ('your scoreboard is as incomplete as mine') but not the operational side ('I can fill every cell today post-hoc, now 95% are yes/yes'). This is the foreseeable author counter-move and the proposal's answer — 'discriminating verdict catches it' — is plausible but not spelled out in the intro copy. See Hole #5.
proposal_package_review.unknowns_acknowledged: `notes_for_human_review_group` (8 items) acknowledges: Phase-1 selection bias (9/15 filled is not representative); 'disputed' semantic overload; eclipse-row duplication (pred-eclipse-tier3 vs killshot-eclipse-ebr); NMP/SAA provenance-unclear rows; tab-label choice; formula-script directory location. This is unusually good self-disclosure. One unknown NOT flagged: the `discriminating` threshold is undefined — see Hole #3.

holes_found.description: Provenance-tag classification rule is subjective and has no operational test. `forward_dome_native` vs `forward_with_globe_inputs` turns on whether a transform is 'near-identity' or 'non-trivial'. The theta-east-longitude.js implementation hardcodes `isNearIdentityTransform() { return true; }` — it is not a general test, just a per-row boolean assertion. Two independent reviewers could classify the same row differently with no tiebreaker. In Phase 1, 5 of 15 rows carry `forward_with_globe_inputs` — if the author disputes even one classification, the pattern claim weakens. The analyst flags this in counter_considerations (#4: 'whether a transform is near-identity requires judgement') but does not operationalise the test.
holes_found.recommendation: Before Phase 1 publication, specify a classification rule with at least two concrete criteria. Suggested minimum: (i) a transform T: globe-inputs → dome-prediction is near-identity iff T reduces to affine form (sign-flip, constant offset, linear scale) OR iff T's dome-specific parameters are themselves fit to globe outputs; (ii) any reviewer invoking the `forward_dome_native` label must point to a dome-native parameter (λ_g, disc_radius, firmament_height) that appears with a value NOT derivable from a globe measurement. State this explicitly in the intro copy so readers can adjudicate.

holes_found.description: Sydney-Perth formula-check implementation cites 'V51.1 paper §4.3.1' for LAMBDA_G_INNER=1.000 and LAMBDA_G_OUTER=1.007. V51.1 is the site, not a numbered-section paper; no such §4.3.1 is independently verifiable. The constants were — per the analyst's own provenance_note — tuned to reproduce 4,352 km. So the formula-check on Sydney-Perth demonstrates provenance=fit BY CONSTRUCTION: the script reproduces 4,352 km because LAMBDA_G_OUTER was set to produce 4,352 km. This is the intended demonstration (the whole point is to show fit), but the cited section reference invents an artefact that doesn't exist, and a dome defender can point at the phantom citation to impeach the whole formula-check concept.
holes_found.recommendation: (a) Replace the '§4.3.1' citation with a verifiable source — either a specific page/equation on the dome site, a commit-dated archive, or 'derived by us from curve-fitting EXP-085/091 corridor data' — and label it honestly. (b) State in the formula-script README that on fit rows the formula-check does not validate the V51.1 derivation; it encodes the observed value and demonstrates that the fit reproduces its input. (c) Consider dropping the formula-check column entirely for rows where provenance=fit — the round-trip is tautological.

holes_found.description: The `discriminating` verdict has no specified numerical threshold. `rounding_tolerance_applied` is defined for `formula_reproduces` but not for 'dome value differs from globe value'. Sydney-Perth has `discriminating: no` because globe=4352 km and dome=4352 km are equal within 0.1%. But what if a future row has globe=31.46' to 32.53' (range) and dome=32.0' (single value within range)? Is that discriminating? The spec does not say. Without a defined threshold, `discriminating` reduces to analyst judgement, and the column loses defensibility.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a `discriminating_threshold` field (or a fixed rule in the intro) parallel to `rounding_tolerance_applied`. Suggested default: `discriminating=yes` iff dome value is outside the globe prediction's stated uncertainty interval (not the dome rounding). Where the globe prediction is a range rather than a point, `discriminating=yes` iff dome is outside the range by more than the instrument resolution. Where globe is qualitative, `discriminating` falls back to reasoned analyst judgement documented in row_notes.

holes_found.description: `disputed` is used in two semantically distinct ways in Phase 1. (a) Killshot-polaris and killshot-sydney-perth: 'author demoted mid-review, pre- vs post-demotion text differs' — this is author-framing-instability. (b) Killshot-nmp-drift, killshot-saa, killshot-jfklhr: 'provenance unclear / author did not publish discriminating numerical prediction' — this is missing-prediction. Both surface as `win_by_own_standard=disputed` and `discriminating=disputed`, which conflates two different kinds of weakness. A reader cannot tell from the verdict column alone which failure mode applies, and the row_notes are the only disambiguator. The analyst flags this in notes_for_human_review_group #2 (`rename 'dispute' to 'author-framing-unstable'?`) but leaves the decision to humans.
holes_found.recommendation: Split `disputed` into two values before Phase 1 publication: `framing_unstable` (author changed labels mid-review) and `disputed` (author and reviewer disagree about whether criteria were met). Or: keep `disputed` but add a required `dispute_type` enum alongside. Either option removes the ambiguity without destroying the five-value schema.

holes_found.description: The proposal's handling of the author's foreseeable counter-move is under-specified. The author could respond to 60 blank cells by going through and writing plausible numbers into each WIN retroactively. The intro paragraph says 'We will update and date-stamp any additions' — which is exactly what a defender wants, because post-hoc filled cells with `win_by_own_standard=yes` read as dome wins to casual readers. The `discriminating` column and `provenance=fit/unclear` tags are the actual defense here, but neither is highlighted in the intro TLDR. A reader who only reads the summary sees 'author filled 60 more cells, dome now wins 90% of rows' unless the intro makes clear that `win_by_own_standard=yes` alone is not a model validation.
holes_found.recommendation: Strengthen the intro paragraph to state explicitly: 'win_by_own_standard=yes alone is NOT evidence the dome model is correct — it only confirms the author counted a row as a WIN by his own definition. The row matters to model evaluation only when discriminating=yes AND provenance is forward_dome_native (not fit or forward_with_globe_inputs) AND the prediction was timestamped before the observation arrived (see Timestamp Error tab).' This is the three-filter rule the scoreboard IMPLIES; make it explicit so author-fills-cells response does not produce a misleading headline.

holes_found.description: Data structure has two parallel layers: `scoreboard` (null-initialized placeholder attached to each row) and `scoreboard_overrides` (populated content). The relationship between the two is not specified. Is `scoreboard_overrides` merged INTO `scoreboard` at build time? Or is the overrides object the canonical source and `scoreboard` a backward-compat placeholder? If both can be written independently, two writers could disagree. For a schema change that will touch every WIN/prediction/kill-shot row, having one source of truth is essential.
holes_found.recommendation: Collapse to a single `scoreboard` object on each row. If the intent is 'scoreboard starts null and becomes populated after curmudgeon pre-review', encode that via the existing `scoreboard_last_updated_by` field (null = unpopulated, 'analyst/curmudgeon/decider' = populated). Delete the `scoreboard_overrides` layer before integration. Alternative: if the dual-layer is load-bearing (e.g., `scoreboard_overrides` holds promoted content and `scoreboard` is the previous promoted snapshot for diffing), spec it explicitly.

holes_found.description: The Tesla-wave-speed row provenance is classed as `forward_with_globe_inputs` with the note that '1.574c numerical agreement reproduces Tesla's measured value BY CONSTRUCTION because the disc circumference IS the globe circumference.' This is correct but buries a rhetorically important subtlety: Tesla's 1.574c is established waveguide physics that REQUIRES a spherical Earth. If the dome model claims this as a WIN, the dome claims a number that only exists because the underlying geometry is spherical — i.e., the row is self-defeating on its face. The scoreboard surfaces this only in the row_notes; a reader who skims the verdict columns sees `win_by_own_standard=yes, discriminating=no` and moves on.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a sentinel flag or badge for 'dome claim presupposes globe geometry' — the geometry-pollution case. This is a stronger category than provenance=forward_with_globe_inputs and worth distinguishing. Tesla 1.574c, θ=−lonE, and potentially Sydney-Perth all qualify. Optional for Phase 1, but worth human-group discussion.

holes_found.description: Build-pipeline integration: the `table_generator_contract` specifies a new helper `generateScoreboardTable(wins, predictions, killshots)` in `build-scripts/generate-html.js` but does not specify how the new `data/killshots.json` file will be created, maintained, or kept consistent with the kill-shot narrative already in `data/sections.json` (Part 5). Kill-shots currently live as prose inside sections; pulling them out into a data file is a data-model change beyond just adding scoreboard cells, and the proposal does not explicitly name that work.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a Phase-1 sub-task: 'Create `data/killshots.json` with the 6 current kill-shot rows extracted from Part 5 prose, and update ownership table in CLAUDE.md and build.js OWNERSHIP to classify the new file as `git-owned` (data file). Make Part 5 HTML generator read from both data/sections.json and data/killshots.json.' Alternatively: scoreboard Phase 1 only extends wins.json and predictions.json; kill-shot rows are tagged via a computed view over existing section data. Either path is defensible, but the current spec does not choose.

holes_found.description: pred-eclipse-tier3 row_notes says: 'NO SHA-256-anchored prediction document exists separately timestamped before Aug 12 2026 as of 2026-04-20.' The word 'SHA-256-anchored' risks conflation with the canary (see CLAUDE.md: SHA-256 per-prediction hashes are distinct from OTS timestamping and do NOT fix the canary). The canary fix requires independent OTS anchoring of a SEPARATE prediction document — not just a SHA-256 hash. Scoreboard cross-ref to Timestamp Error tab is present, but this line alone could mislead.
holes_found.recommendation: Change 'NO SHA-256-anchored prediction document exists separately timestamped' to 'NO independently OTS-timestamped prediction document has been published separately from the observation file as of 2026-04-20 (see Timestamp Error tab for the methodological distinction between per-prediction hashing and independent prospective anchoring).' Minor copy fix, preserves the canary clarity.

stronger_arguments.description: The scoreboard's strongest latent argument is the ASYMMETRY of blank cells. Of ~85 total potentially-scorable rows (71 WINs + ~8 predictions + 6 kill-shots), Phase 2 will reveal that the vast majority have no published dome numerical prediction. The 'author's definition of a WIN is confirmed prediction; a missing prediction cannot be confirmed' framing is devastating if delivered with the full-universe ratio. Phase 1's 9/15=60% filled ratio UNDERSELLS the scoreboard's eventual power because Phase 1 selected exactly the rows the author attended to most. The intro paragraph should state Phase 2 expectations explicitly: 'At Phase 1 publication, 9 of 15 selected rows carry a published dome number. Phase 2 will extend to the full row universe; the filled-ratio is expected to drop significantly. We will publish the final ratio when Phase 2 completes.'

The scoreboard converts the 'vendor lock-in' critique from Part 4 (Live Power Dashboard) into row-level evidence. Part 4 says '67 WINs were retrofit post-observation'; the scoreboard says 'for each of those WINs, here is the globe prediction, here is the dome prediction as published (blank for most), here is the observation.' The scoreboard IS the testable version of Part 4's aggregate claim. The proposal under-emphasizes this — integration_points notes 'Part 4 continues' without acknowledging the scoreboard is the per-row corroborator of Part 4's aggregate pattern.

The pre-registration surface for pred-eclipse-tier3 is a serious pre-commitment mechanism that the proposal slightly undersells. Publishing a scoreboard row with explicitly BLANK Tier-3 threshold cells, cross-linked to the Timestamp Error tab, means that on August 11 2026 the row will either (a) still be blank, which is itself a finding about the August 12 eclipse predictions' operationalization status, or (b) filled by an author publication that we can then evaluate. Either outcome strengthens our review. Human group should consider whether to replicate this pre-registration surface on other pending kill-shot rows (NMP drift by 2027, JFK-LHR aetheric slipstream) to turn more rows into date-stamped author opportunities.

kernel_of_truth.description: The analyst has done unusually careful work. The proposal anticipates 8 objections, lists 5 counter-considerations, and flags 8 unknowns for human-group review — this is what high-quality adversarial preparation looks like. The dual-columns design (win_by_own_standard + discriminating) is a genuine methodological contribution: it separates 'did the author count this as a WIN?' from 'does this WIN distinguish the dome model from the globe model?' — the second question is the one that matters for model evaluation, and most writing on this topic elides it. The provenance labels (fit/forward_dome_native/forward_with_globe_inputs/unclear) are a real taxonomy improvement over the binary 'predicted vs not'. And the guardrail 'never derive dome numbers for the author' is the correct methodological stance.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Does not apply — this is a proposal review, not a WIN review. The kernel of truth here is evaluative of the proposal, not adversarial. The proposal is strong work; the holes above are refinements, not refutations.

recommended_action_notes: This is a FOUNDATIONAL proposal review for human-group consumption. The decider is blocked from integration until operator posts HNOTE-SCOREBOARD-INTEGRATION-APPROVED-001 (per queue item #194 `integration_blocked_until_operator_hnote`). My recommendation to the human group: proceed with Phase 1 integration AFTER addressing Holes H1-H6 (the majors and first four moderates). H7-H9 can be deferred to Phase 2 or handled as copy fixes during integration. The core design is sound; the holes are implementation polish.
pre_integration_checklist: H1: Specify provenance classification rule with two concrete criteria in intro + formula-script README.
H2: Replace phantom '§4.3.1' citation; add 'on fit rows the formula-check reproduces its own input' disclaimer.
H3: Add discriminating_threshold rule (dome outside globe uncertainty interval) to spec.
H4: Split `disputed` into framing_unstable + disputed, or add dispute_type enum.
H5: Strengthen intro TLDR with the three-filter rule (discriminating AND forward_dome_native AND timestamped).
H6: Collapse scoreboard + scoreboard_overrides into single source of truth.
H7: Optional — add geometry-pollution flag for Tesla/θ=−lonE/Sydney-Perth rows.
H8: Spec killshots.json creation/ownership or alternative computed-view approach.
H9: Fix SHA-256 vs OTS wording on pred-eclipse-tier3 row_notes.

text_fingerprint.verdict: approve_with_modifications (advisory — integration gated by operator HNOTE)

citations_verified: EXP-188 (Sydney-Perth and Polaris kill-shot demotions) — confirmed verbatim in EXP-188.json including commit 5021eec and 2026-04-17 date
HNOTE-SCOREBOARD-PIPELINE-ROUTING-001 — referenced correctly per queue item #194
claim_index.json reference to 9 classified rows — consistent with analyst_notes
citations_failed: Sydney-Perth formula-check cites 'V51.1 paper §4.3.1' — no such numbered section exists in V51.1 site materials (Hole H2).

EXP-085/EXP-091 cited as archival evidence that Sydney-Perth was input to the λ_g fit — EXP-085 documents the pattern 'dome repackages own parameters' but does not explicitly say Sydney-Perth distance was an input to the λ_g fit. Analyst's claim may be correct but needs tighter sourcing.
citations_unchecked: Tesla 1899 Colorado Springs measurements / US Patent 787,412 — not verified by me this run
IGRF-14 / WMM 2020 NMP drift ratio of ~1.4 — cited in row_notes but not independently checked
Geoscience Australia cadastral geodesy Sydney-Perth 4352 km ±5 km — accepted at face value

questions_for_human_review_group: Do we accept the 'Scoreboard' tab label, or prefer the more neutral 'Both-Model Predictions'? Tab label is load-bearing for navigation and has rhetorical weight.
Should Phase 1 include 2-3 randomly-selected unclassified WINs (analyst's suggestion #3) to avoid sampling-bias optics, or accept that Phase 1 intentionally showcases the author-attended rows?
Should `disputed` be split into `framing_unstable` and `disputed` (Hole H4), or kept unified with a sub-type field?
Do we require Holes H1-H6 resolved BEFORE Phase 1 publication, or can H5/H6 slip to mid-Phase-1 iteration?
Should we replicate pred-eclipse-tier3's pre-registration surface design on NMP drift (2027 window) and JFK-LHR (ongoing) rows?
